1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). But for line bundles, the comparison map is the oplax \(\delta\) of the left
96	adjoint, produced by \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
97	(\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}) applied to the
98	pullback--pushforward adjunction on presheaves of modules; the sole
99	project-side ingredient is the sectionwise
100	\((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}\) instance, and
101	flatness of line bundles then makes \(\delta\) an isomorphism. This is
102	exactly the carrier on which the relative Picard group law of
103	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} rests, and it is the subject of
104	\cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
105	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
106	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
107	
108	\section{Mathlib API survey}
109	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
110	
111	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
112	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
113	
114	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
115	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
116	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
118	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
119	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
120	sectionwise tensor product
121	\[
122	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
123	\]
124	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
125	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
126	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
127	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
128	
129	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
131	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
132	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
133	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
134	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
135	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
136	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
137	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
138	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
139	tensor-product operation
140	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
141	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
142	
143	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
144	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
145	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
146	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
147	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
148	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
149	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
150	\emph{flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
151	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
152	for line bundles \(L, M\) and the restriction along an open immersion
153	\(f\), the canonical comparison
154	\((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes M|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
155	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
156	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume; for line bundles, the comparison
157	map is the oplax \(\delta\) of \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) applied to
158	the pullback--pushforward adjunction, with the sole project-side ingredient the
159	sectionwise \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}\) instance,
160	and flatness of line bundles then upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism
161	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
162	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
163	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
164	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
165	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
166	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
167	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
168	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
169	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
170	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
171	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
172	independently of any upstream timing.
173	
174	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
175	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
176	
177	\begin{definition}
178	
179	\leanok
180	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
181	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
182	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
183	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
184	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
185	  \[
186	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
187	  \]
188	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
189	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
190	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
191	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
192	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
193	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
194	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
195	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
196	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
197	  canonical compatibility
198	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
199	     \;\cong\;
200	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
201	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
202	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
203	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
204	
205	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
206	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
207	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
208	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
209	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
210	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
211	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
212	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
213	\end{definition}
214	
215	\begin{lemma}
216	
217	\leanok
218	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
219	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
221	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
222	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
223	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
224	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
225	  \[
226	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
227	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
228	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
229	  \]
230	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
231	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
232	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
233	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
234	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
235	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
236	  \[
237	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
238	    \qquad
239	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
240	  \]
241	  the associator
242	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
243	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
244	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
245	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
246	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
247	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
252	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
253	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
254	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
255	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
256	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
257	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
258	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
259	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
260	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
261	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
262	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
263	\end{proof}
264	
265	\begin{remark}
266	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
267	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
268	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
269	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
270	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
271	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
272	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
273	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
274	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
275	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
276	  depend on it.
277	
278	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
280	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
281	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
282	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
283	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
284	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
285	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
286	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
287	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
288	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
289	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
290	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
291	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
292	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
293	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
294	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
295	
296	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
297	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the flat-scoped
298	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
299	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
300	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
301	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
302	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
303	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
304	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
305	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
306	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
307	\end{remark}

309	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
310	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
311	
312	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
313	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
314	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
315	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
316	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
317	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility
318	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
319	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
320	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
321	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
322	cover, together with their restriction to the
323	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
324	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
325	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
326	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
330	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
331	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
332	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
333	  % NOTE (iter-207 review): formalized axiom-clean as the instance
334	  % `PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` (with helpers
335	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`). HOWEVER the second half of
336	  % this lemma's claim --- that this instance "supplies the hypothesis ...
337	  % required by the mate construction" and is "the SOLE genuinely project-side
338	  % ingredient" of `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` --- was DISPROVEN this iter; see
339	  % the % NOTE on lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso below.
340	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
341	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
342	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
343	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
344	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
345	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
346	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
347	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
348	  \[
349	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
350	      \;\;:=\;\;
351	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
352	  \]
353	  is lax monoidal. This supplies the hypothesis
354	  \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\) required by the
355	  mate construction \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) in the proof of
356	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and is the \emph{sole} genuinely
357	  project-side ingredient that construction depends on.
358	\end{lemma}
359	
360	\begin{proof}
361	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
362	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
363	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
364	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
365	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
366	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
367	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
368	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
369	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
370	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
371	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
372	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
373	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
374	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
375	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
376	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
377	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
378	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
379	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
380	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
381	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
382	
383	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
384	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
385	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
386	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
387	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
388	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
389	\end{proof}
390	
391	\begin{lemma}
392	
393	\leanok
394	[Tensor product commutes with restriction for line bundles]
395	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
396	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
397	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
398	  % NOTE (iter-207 review): the proof below and the comment block that follows
399	  % assert that the sectionwise lax instance (lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal,
400	  % now built) is the SOLE remaining project-side ingredient and that closing
401	  % this iso is "a bounded mathlib-build target, not a multi-file wall." That is
402	  % NOT formalizable as written. The prover built the lax instance axiom-clean,
403	  % yet `tensorObj_restrict_iso` remained blocked: (1) the scheme module ring

407	  % and the tensorObj definition live at different ring layers; (2) at the

409	  % composed by instance resolution (a `⋙`-vs-`forget₂` associativity mismatch).

411	  % CommRingCat layer, OR add line-bundle hypotheses and prove sectionwise via
412	  % `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`), NOT one further instance. See
413	  % task_results/AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.md (iter-207).
414	  % NOTE: the comparison map below is NOT built by hand. It is the
415	  % oplax-monoidal comparison δ of the left adjoint `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ`,
416	  % obtained from the already-present Mathlib construction
417	  % `CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` applied to the present
418	  % adjunction `PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ`. That
419	  % construction's sole hypothesis is `[(pushforward φ).LaxMonoidal]`, and the
420	  % SOLE remaining project-side ingredient is the sectionwise lax-monoidal
421	  % instance of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. This is a bounded
422	  % `mathlib-build` target (one sectionwise lax instance, ~40--90 LOC), not a
423	  % multi-file wall: every other piece (the mate δ, the `comp`, the flat-exactness
424	  % upgrade to an iso) is already in Mathlib.
425	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be line
426	  bundles (equivalently: locally-trivial rank-one modules, hence flat), and
427	  let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the restriction along an open immersion.
428	  The canonical comparison morphism
429	  \[
430	    (L \otimes_X M)\big|_f
431	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
432	    L\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; M\big|_f
433	  \]
434	  is an isomorphism. The intended Lean target is
435	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
436	\end{lemma}
437	
438	\begin{proof}
439	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
440	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of (commutative) structure-sheaf-valued
441	  presheaves of rings induced by the open immersion \(f\), so that
442	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor
443	  on presheaves of modules. The proof proceeds by first \emph{constructing}
444	  the comparison morphism as the canonical base-change map of a left adjoint,
445	  and only then upgrading it to an isomorphism using flatness.
446	
447	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The
448	  restriction \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion
449	  \(f\) is naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
450	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{obj}(-)\),
451	  via the present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\).
452	  It therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism
453	  for the abstract pullback functor.
454	
455	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
456	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
457	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
458	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
459	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
460	   \;\cong\;
461	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
462	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence
463	  the whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the
464	  base-change comparison
465	  \[
466	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
467	      \;\longrightarrow\;
468	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
469	      \;\otimes\;
470	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
471	  \]
472	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\).
473	
474	  \emph{Step 3 (the comparison map is the mate of pushforward).} The functor
475	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is a left adjoint --- it is
476	  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\)
477	  through the present adjunction
478	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi
479	    : \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
480	  The base-change comparison of Step 2 is exactly the oplax-monoidal
481	  comparison \(\delta\) of this left adjoint, obtained by the mate
482	  construction \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
483	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}), whose data are
484	  \(\delta_{A,B} = (\mathrm{adj}.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(
485	    (\eta_A \otimes \eta_B) \circ \mu_{\cdot}\bigr)\) for the unit \(\eta\) of
486	  the adjunction and the lax structure \(\mu\) of the right adjoint. The sole
487	  hypothesis of that construction is that the right adjoint be lax monoidal,
488	  i.e.\ \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\); this is
489	  furnished by \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. The comparison map is
490	  thus produced \emph{abstractly}, with no sectionwise formula required.
491	
492	  \emph{Step 4 (flatness upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism).} It remains
493	  to check that, for line bundles, this already-constructed \(\delta\) is an
494	  isomorphism --- and \emph{this} is where flatness enters. The check is
495	  local, so it suffices to verify it sectionwise, where the comparison is the
496	  ordinary base-change map of modules along a ring map and \(\delta\)
497	  specialises to \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,(-)\) applied to the
498	  structural comparison for the second factor. A line bundle is locally free
499	  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
500	  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
501	  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\).
502	  Tensor is always right-exact, so \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity,
503	  and flatness of \(L\) makes \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve
504	  injectivity (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
505	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
506	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
507	  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
508	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
509	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). We stress that this step
510	  \emph{upgrades} the comparison \(\delta\) of Step 3 to an isomorphism; it
511	  does \emph{not} construct the map, and is not a substitute for Step 3.
512	
513	  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise/affine isomorphisms of Step 4 are compatible
514	  with the restriction maps, so by \(\mathtt{hom\_ext}\) they assemble into a
515	  single isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules; transporting back through
516	  the isomorphisms of Steps 1--2 yields the asserted global isomorphism
517	  \((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes_U M|_f\).
518	\end{proof}

13	Lane TS's abstract-adjoint comparison-map (mate δ) route for
14	`tensorObj_restrict_iso` is definitively blocked (it needs the absent
15	`(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ).Monoidal`). Which re-route is the
16	Mathlib-idiomatic, *bounded* one — (A) exploit that `f` is an open immersion
17	(sectionwise restriction + base-change-along-a-ring-iso), or (B) add
18	`IsLocallyTrivial` hypotheses and glue local isos — and do its prerequisites
19	exist at `b80f227` or are they an absent multi-file build?

21	## Project artifact(s)
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:330-367` — `tensorObj_restrict_iso`
23	  (current body: 2 real reduction steps + residual `sorry`).

25	  `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` instance built for the now-dead δ-route.

29	## Key mathematical fact the routes hinge on
30	
31	`tensorObj_restrict_iso` is **TRUE for arbitrary `M N : X.Modules`** — tensor
32	product commutes with restriction to an open subscheme with *no* local-freeness
33	hypothesis. Restriction along an open immersion is exact and is base change
34	along the structure-sheaf **isomorphism** `f.appIso`, not a general flat ring
35	map. This single observation reshapes the whole decision: Route (B)'s
36	`IsLocallyTrivial` hypotheses are mathematically *unnecessary* for this lemma,
37	and the δ-route's `extendScalars`-monoidality (needed only because it modeled
38	restriction as a *general* base change) is overkill.
39	
40	## Decisions identified
41	
42	### Decision A: Route (A) — open-immersion sectionwise / base-change-along-a-ring-iso
43	
44	- **Mathlib idiom**: restriction along an open immersion is the **concrete,
45	  definitionally sectionwise** functor `Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor`, NOT the
46	  abstract pullback. The load-bearing facts are all present at `b80f227`:
47	  - `restrict_obj` / `restrict_map` (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean:328,331`)
48	    — **both `rfl`**: `Γ(M.restrict f, U) = Γ(M, f ''ᵁ U)`. Restriction = reindex
49	    sections along `f.opensFunctor`.
50	  - `Scheme.Hom.appIso (U) : Γ(Y, f ''ᵁ U) ≅ Γ(X, U)`
51	    (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/OpenImmersion.lean:190`) — the `CommRingCat`
52	    isomorphism along which `restrictFunctor` reindexes scalars (it is literally
53	    `α.app U := (f.appIso U.unop).inv` in `restrictFunctor`'s def, Sheaf.lean:320).
54	  - `ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv (e : R ≃+* S)`
55	    (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/ChangeOfRings.lean:285`) — base change
56	    along a **ring iso** is an equivalence (the "extension of scalars along an
57	    iso is trivially iso" the directive asks for). PRESENT.
58	  - `PresheafOfModules.isoMk` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf.lean:118`)
59	    — assemble a sectionwise `app + naturality` into a presheaf-of-modules iso.

61	    (Sheaf.lean:132) — `IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U)`; plus the stalkwise

89	### Decision B: Route (B) — add `IsLocallyTrivial` and glue local isos
90	
91	- **Mathlib idiom**: there is **no `SheafOfModules` gluing primitive** for
92	  assembling local isos into a global iso. The idiomatic move is the reverse:
93	  build a *global canonical morphism* first, then check it is an iso *locally*
94	  (`isIso_iff_isIso_app` per open, or `isIso_iff_stalkFunctor_map_iso` per
95	  stalk). "Build locally then glue the morphism" forces a cocycle/overlap
96	  agreement check with no off-the-shelf support.
97	- **Project's path (proposed)**: add `(hM hN : IsLocallyTrivial …)`, trivialise
98	  on a common affine `W`, build the iso there, glue.
99	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost on two counts. (1) The hypotheses are
100	  **mathematically unnecessary** — the lemma holds for arbitrary `M, N`
101	  (tensor commutes with open restriction), so adding them is a false constraint
102	  that future general consumers cannot satisfy. (2) Even with them, you still
103	  need a *global* comparison morphism to apply any iso criterion, so the
104	  triviality buys you nothing the global-morphism-checked-locally route (A)
105	  doesn't already give for free — and the "glue local isos" step is strictly
106	  *more* work than (A) (cocycle bookkeeping with no primitive).
107	- **Verdict**: **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY = do NOT take (B)**. It is heavier than

185	### Decision 4: `presheaf` vs `sheaf` and the non-affine-V hazard
186	
187	This is a CRITICAL observation the iter-133 verdict did not surface
188	explicitly.

190	- The project's `Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f` is built on
191	  `PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`,

193	  `Ω[Γ(X,V) ⁄ (f⁻¹ O_S)(V)]` — i.e. Kähler differentials of an
194	  algebra map that, for non-affine V, has no particular
195	  geometric interpretation. The presheaf is `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf`
196	  not `relativeDifferentialsSheaf`; there is no sheafification.

198	- For affine V, both sides are computable Kähler modules of the
199	  natural algebra map (and `tensorKaehlerEquiv` connects them).
200	  For non-affine V, both sides are still defined (as Kähler modules
201	  of the corresponding non-affine ring map), but
202	  `tensorKaehlerEquiv`'s hypothesis (`Algebra.IsPushout`) is not
203	  generally satisfied on non-affine opens — affine pushout-of-rings
204	  is a property special to spectra, not arbitrary opens.
205	
206	- **Implication**: the chart-wise `app` of `isoMk` cannot just be
207	  "`tensorKaehlerEquiv` of the V-sections"; it must be derived
208	  from the universal property of Kähler differentials at the
209	  presheaf level. Concretely: define a `Derivation` of the LHS
210	  ring map into the RHS module at the presheaf level (one
211	  derivation per open V, plus naturality across V → W restrictions),
212	  invoke `KaehlerDifferential.lift` to produce the LHS-to-RHS map,
213	  and analogously construct the inverse. This is the same strategy
214	  Mathlib uses to construct `tensorKaehlerEquiv` itself
215	  (`Kaehler/TensorProduct.lean:225–283`), lifted from a ring to a
216	  presheaf of rings.

218	- The alternative (affine-cover-and-glue + sheafification) requires
219	  the project to either (i) sheafify `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf`
220	  (which is itself a non-trivial separate piece of infrastructure)
221	  or (ii) restrict to the affine-open subcategory before applying
222	  `isoMk`. Both are heavier than the universal-property-at-presheaf
223	  approach.

226	  (which the blueprint at `RigidityKbar.tex:474–478` hints at) is
227	  not directly executable on `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` since

231	  approach; the chart-affine-cover narrative in the blueprint is

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of (commutative) structure-sheaf-valued
  presheaves of rings induced by the open immersion \(f\), so that
  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor
  on presheaves of modules. The proof proceeds by first \emph{constructing}
  the comparison morphism as the canonical base-change map of a left adjoint,
  and only then upgrading it to an isomorphism using flatness.

  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The
  restriction \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion
  \(f\) is naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{obj}(-)\),
  via the present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\).
  It therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism
  for the abstract pullback functor.

  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
   \;\cong\;
   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence
  the whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the
  base-change comparison
  \[
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
      \;\otimes\;
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
  \]
  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\).

  \emph{Step 3 (the comparison map is the mate of pushforward).} The functor
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is a left adjoint --- it is
  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\)
  through the present adjunction
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi
    : \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
  The base-change comparison of Step 2 is exactly the oplax-monoidal
  comparison \(\delta\) of this left adjoint, obtained by the mate
  construction \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}), whose data are
  \(\delta_{A,B} = (\mathrm{adj}.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(
    (\eta_A \otimes \eta_B) \circ \mu_{\cdot}\bigr)\) for the unit \(\eta\) of
  the adjunction and the lax structure \(\mu\) of the right adjoint. The sole
  hypothesis of that construction is that the right adjoint be lax monoidal,
  i.e.\ \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\); this is
  furnished by \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. The comparison map is
  thus produced \emph{abstractly}, with no sectionwise formula required.

  \emph{Step 4 (flatness upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism).} It remains
  to check that, for line bundles, this already-constructed \(\delta\) is an
  isomorphism --- and \emph{this} is where flatness enters. The check is
  local, so it suffices to verify it sectionwise, where the comparison is the
  ordinary base-change map of modules along a ring map and \(\delta\)
  specialises to \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,(-)\) applied to the
  structural comparison for the second factor. A line bundle is locally free
  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\).
  Tensor is always right-exact, so \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity,
  and flatness of \(L\) makes \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve
  injectivity (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). We stress that this step
  \emph{upgrades} the comparison \(\delta\) of Step 3 to an isomorphism; it
  does \emph{not} construct the map, and is not a substitute for Step 3.

  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise/affine isomorphisms of Step 4 are compatible
  with the restriction maps, so by \(\mathtt{hom\_ext}\) they assemble into a
  single isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules; transporting back through
  the isomorphisms of Steps 1--2 yields the asserted global isomorphism
  \((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes_U M|_f\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
  isomorphism}
  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
  \Gamma(U, V)\) of \cite-free Mathlib origin
  (\(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\), a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
  isomorphism). Restriction along \(f\) is therefore extension of scalars along
  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.

  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
  the abstract pullback functor.

  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
   \;\cong\;
   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
  comparison
  \[
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
      \;\otimes\;
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
  \]
  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).

  \emph{Step 3 (sectionwise identification along the open-immersion ring
  isomorphism).} The functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is
  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of pushforward and is consequently
  \emph{opaque} on objects and morphisms: Mathlib provides no sectionwise
  formula for it, and --- unlike the scheme-level restriction --- no
  presheaf-level analogue of \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). The one
  genuinely-new project-side ingredient of this proof is therefore a
  \emph{sectionwise unfolding} of the abstract pullback along the open
  immersion: for the open-immersion ring morphism \(\varphi\), the presheaf
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,P\) is, section by section over an
  open \(V\), the base change of the sections of \(P\) along the ring
  \emph{isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\) --- that is, restriction of
  sections combined with scalar transport across the isomorphism. (Over an open
  immersion the sections themselves agree definitionally,
  \(\Gamma(M|_f, V) = \Gamma(M, f(V))\) by the \(\mathtt{restrict\_obj}\)
  identity; only the scalar ring is transported, and it is transported along an
  isomorphism.)

  Granting this sectionwise description, the base-change comparison of Step 2
  is, on each section over \(V\), the canonical map of a tensor product under
  base change along the ring isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Base change
  along a ring isomorphism is a \emph{monoidal equivalence} of module
  categories --- it is invertible and carries tensor products to tensor products
  --- by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\) applied to
  the ring equivalence underlying \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Hence each
  sectionwise comparison is an isomorphism compatible with \(\otimes\). No
  flatness, no \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)-monoidality, and no strong-monoidal
  pullback instance is invoked: the entire force of the step is that the scalar
  change is along an \emph{isomorphism}.

  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise isomorphisms of Step 3 are natural in \(V\)
  (compatible with the presheaf restriction maps, the naturality square being
  that of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) against restriction). They therefore assemble,
  via \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\), into a single presheaf-of-modules
  isomorphism realising the base-change comparison of Step 2 as an iso.
  Transporting this back through the sheafification units of Step 2 and the
  restriction--pullback comparison of Step 1 yields the asserted global
  isomorphism \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\).

  The route uses no monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) or on the
  pullback functor, no flatness, and no line-bundle hypothesis. The single
  bounded project-side ingredient is the sectionwise unfolding of
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along an open immersion (a
  short \({\sim}30\text{--}60\) line helper, mirroring one layer down the
  scheme-level \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) and with direct precedent
  in the analogous presheaf-pullback unfolding used for the base change of
  Kähler differentials); every other ingredient
  (\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\),
  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\),
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\),
  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\)) is already present in Mathlib.
\end{proof}
